item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it was taken by Robin Lorenson as indicated here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is very wholesome. I want one.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this SCP. Unlike many other objects ,it isn't always in a "horror" mode. Sure, there is horror is the idea that it could cause devastation if it wanted. However, what I find to be the hook is that it just wants to sort of hang around with humans. It's sort of sweet in a way. Of course, if you want you can go in very dark places to explain why is acts the way it does. All in all, a great SCP in my book. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw… a cute little tornado. I agree with the people that said that this scip may be kind of "over-light-hearted" but I don't think that's a really bad thing. It's nice to read and fun. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's odd, because when I opened the editor the text that was missing is actually there, but it wasn't showing up. Nevertheless, I've resolved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Drat, I was about to point that out.

But seriously though, I like it, but that bit there just threw me off majorly. Is there a bit of the interview missing or something? I could approve of a [DATA EXPUNGED] or [REDACTED], but this is just… Missing. And now I want to know what it is that is missing.

Novote, until I can quench my curiosity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice one.
The thing that bugs me, is interviev:

Dr. ████: How did you approach it?

Dr. ████: How hurt was he?

I guess something is missing here.
Anyway, I enjoy this one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm surprised this one doesn't have a higher score. I think it's one of the better executed examples of the surprisingly common "things that aren't pets act like one" type of SCPs. I especially like how the article toys around with how intelligent it is.

I wonder, is there a significance behind the agent referring to SCP-1352 as "he" or "him"? Seems a bit odd for something that technically isn't a living organism and doesn't even have a gender.

I also like how the agents who are working with it seem to be getting a bit casual with it, almost treating it like a friend. Goes to show that when you work for the Foundation, what you consider normal tends to become a bit… different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would pay all the money if this were contained in Australia instead of Nevada, specifically that non-New Zealand island to the south of the main landmass..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love your concept, but as everyone has said the execution really needs work. Still, what you have is goon enough for an upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice idea, but tone in the interview drives it out, but I struggle just as much tone-wise

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You mean Taz right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea. I know this has been said, but show, don't tell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Kitty" or "Kitten" or something along those lines, as best I recall.
It lived in Chicago, made friends with a junkyard gaurd and newspaper reporter, and helped take down a corrupt mayor. Think the story was in the "Expanded Universe" anthology.

For my money, this article isn't badly written at all, but it doesn't expand the concept past what Admiral Bob did. So downvoted for being done before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm trying to remember what the phenomenon was nicknamed in the old Heinlein story I first encountered this idea in.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really find this concept both adorable and endearing, and that means it's definitely working. The problem is that the execution, while presenting it enough to really emphasize what it can do, still doesn't do it all the justice that it could do. Overall it just feels a little bare and I'd like to see you add some more to this, but I'm not sure to suggest.

Other than that, the interview really doesn't add much to this, and the referring to SCP-1352 as 'he' really seems off. While it may be an agent talking, it's overly casual. The dialogue doesn't really seem natural, either. It reads as though you tried to make this conversational in order to make it appear like naturally spoken language, but in that effort you stilted it. There's weird wording and word choice that lends to sounding inconsistent and off. When I see 'anyhoo', 'sucker', and 'ruckus', for example, I immediately feel like it's a fake, scripted conversation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is hard, because I actually like the playful dog-devil dust, and I'd like to have one as a pet. We could probably go out, play, fetch the wood, destroy my enemies, if any of course, and probably I'd find the way to "ride" it. That's exactly why I don't think this works as a skip. It's just the incredible (and OTT) pet.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having read back through this, I like it. A pet dust devil is just great. I'm a little thrown by the Agent calling it "he", but I supposed if the guy's gotten to know it, he could be sympathizing with it more now. Maybe have the interviewer upbraid him for lack of professionalism? Beyond that, there are a few minor tonal things in the article, but on reflection, I don't think that's enough for me not to upvote at this point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I distinctly remember this one, even key points of the document. Did someone do a rewrite of it or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually don't mind the hectare over 0.25 sq. km as a) we did learn them as a metric unit in school and b) it's a marginal bit of information anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
besides, bullets can't do shit on him so why bother. In the end, we managed to lure it into the holding container, but someone had to stay with him, or else he'd get mopy and make another ruckus.

I like how it's not even consistent.
That said, it's just about OK since it's the debriefing of an agent. It'd be worse if it was so in the main document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Patriarchy, obviously
:-P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just out of curiosity, what prompted the assigning of a masculine identity to the sentient atmospheric phenomenon?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Same here, +1

But i think it went a little too far with the "Playful" thing.

It needs to be implied, not forced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He got rid of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the text that appears at the bottom of every comment, if you set it up. They're not allowed on the Foundation wiki. Scantron was addressing Mastersarge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What signature?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please remove your signature. It is against site policy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept of this is ok, but it needs a helluva lot of work on this. The execution wasn't very well thought out.
So I won't rate good nor bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but you need to act upon the suggestions offered.

+1, though. It has potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. ████: Please recall how you captured SCP-1352

Please add a period to this oh god it's killing me.

Neutral for now. The base idea I enjoy, it's mainly the tone in the interview that rubs me the wrong way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. Them actively treating it like a pet is just silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh hey, this is back. Call me crazy, but I like tornado puppies. Do do the polishing up other users have pointed out, but you still get a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, I think this could be good if the Foundation did analysis and found that it wasn't actually displaying signs of intelligence or playfulness, and was just exerting a mental effect on people that causes them to be disposed to viewing its actions in that light. Because the article right now reads that they're actively trying to think of it as a pet, which is kind of silly, because they would do their darnedest to explain it from a neutral standpoint.

E: Author, while this might end up sticking in the low positives (at least for a while before being deleted after a slightly embarrassing slide through the negatives), I strongly recommend doing further revisions of this to accommodate the suggestions people have made, particularly considering that this still has a substantial number of downvotes. This could be a much better SCP than it currently is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show Let's take a look under the hood.
- Well, THERE'S your problem!

SCP-1352's enclosure is located in an isolated valley of the Amargosa Range.

Wikipedia informs me that the Amargosa Range is located in Nevada along the eastern edge of Death Valley, but it shouldn't be assumed that the reader knows that - unless the person reading the article grew up in that region, spends a lot of time there recreationally of professionally, or is the Undertaker, odds are they've never heard of it.

The entity is permitted to wander around the premises of the enclosure.

It's a tornado. How are you going to stop it from roaming? Harsh language? If the idea is to restrict it to this area, you really ought to explain how.

The enclosure is 25 hectares

"Hectare" is not a standard metric unit. Square kilometers are the preferred unit for surface area.

All personnel are allowed to interact with SCP-1352, but only during breaks or otherwise not performing any tasks.

This is just plain silly.

"Hey, boss, can I go play with the tornado?"
"Did you finish your TPS reports?"
"I'll finish 'em after supper! Super-swear!"

but they shift with the "mood" of the entity.

"Mood" does "not" need to "be" in "quotation" marks.

and the entity will attempt surround the person/s

You're missing a word there.

If excited, wind speeds and funnel size rapidly shift between the 60 and 100 km/h parameters. In an angered state, the funnel grows to proportions similar to those of F5 tornadoes. The entity will then attack with debris thrown at speeds excess of 300 km/h.

Remind me, why do we let just anyone wander in and play with the tornado again?

Unusually, the funnel doesn't increase or decrease in size when this occurs

Contractions are bad tone.

and solving simple problems i.e., matching objects.

"I.e." means "that is". You mean to say e.g. - i.e., "for example".

We just played with him like a dog until the Recovery teams showed up, which I think was thirty minutes later. Besides, bullets can't do shit on him so why bother.

Again, you really need to explain what a "Recovery Team" does to lure a tornado into captivity. Did they lay down a trail of Beggin' Strips or something?

Final thought: This is actually a good idea that I'd like to see improved upon, but as it is the writing is just too sloppy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kinda bland, in my opinion, and the interview could use a lot of polishing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, the interview log was not a strong point in the article. Also, the idea as a whole reminds me of those twisters in old Looney Toons episodes that follow the characters around. Not too great…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the idea of this, but the tone of the interview threw me off. The agent's narrative voice vacillates between formal and informal so often that it disrupts the story. But I do think you have a cool idea so no vote for now.
